17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).

30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple

42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.

79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. 88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.

Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. 1	\chapter{Coherence of locally trivial line bundles}
2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundleCoherence}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundleCoherence.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter supplies the cheap, on-path half of the
6	% line-bundle coherence requirement of the A.2.c Quot-scheme embedding
7	% (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}).
8	% The Quot functor takes coherent quotients of a coherent sheaf; to feed it a
9	% line bundle one must know that the line bundle is itself coherent, i.e. a
10	% finitely presented \(\mathcal{O}\)-module. The embedding's input is a point of
11	% the relative Picard scheme \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), whose carrier is the
12	% \emph{locally trivial} predicate \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) of
13	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (the project-side stand-in for an
14	% invertible sheaf, a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules locally isomorphic to
15	% \(\mathcal{O}\) of rank one). The chapter proves
16	% \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial} M \Rightarrow M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\),
17	% together with a rank-one / flatness record. The general implication
18	% ``\(M\) tensor-invertible \(\Rightarrow\) \(M\) locally free of rank one''
19	% (the converse spreading-out, Stacks Lemma~0B8M when stalks are local) is
20	% \emph{not} crossed here: the carrier is already locally trivial, so the
21	% finite-presentation spreading-out is never needed.
22	
23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:lbc_setup}
25	
26	Fix a scheme \(X\) and write \(X.\mathtt{Modules}\) for the category of sheaves
27	of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) on the
28	ringed-space site of \(X\), with structure sheaf \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)).
29	A \emph{line bundle} on \(X\) is an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module; in the
30	sense of \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} we model line bundles by the
31	predicate \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}\): a module \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) is locally
32	trivial of rank one if every point \(x \in X\) has an \emph{affine} open
33	neighbourhood \(U\) on which the restriction \(M|_U\) is isomorphic, as an
34	\(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, to the structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U}\):
35	\[
36	  \texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M) \;:=\;
37	  \forall x \in X,\ \exists\, U \ni x \text{ affine open},\quad
38	  M|_U \;\cong\; \struct{U}.
39	\]
40	This is the rank-one case of Stacks Definition~17.25.1
41	(Tag~01CS, invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules), and
42	agrees with the classical ``locally free of rank one'' description by Stacks
43	Lemma~17.25.4 (Tag~0B8M).
44	
45	The goal of the chapter is to record that such a module is \emph{coherent} in the
46	sense the Quot construction consumes, namely finitely presented:
47	\(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) in Mathlib's
48	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) (built from a
49	\(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\)). The mathematics is elementary: on each
50	trivialising chart \(M|_U \cong \struct{U}\) is finite free of rank one, hence
51	has the trivial finite presentation, and finite presentation is by definition a
52	local condition. No stalk computation and no spreading-out are involved. The
53	content is the bookkeeping that turns the pointwise trivialisation into the
54	indexed \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) Mathlib expects.
55	
56	\section{Site instances for quasi-coherent data}
57	\label{sec:lbc_site_instances}
58	
59	% NOTE (chief unknown). Mathlib's SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation /
60	% QuasicoherentData are stated under three site hypotheses on the slice
61	% topologies J.over X of the underlying site J of X.ringCatSheaf:
62	%   [∀ x, (J.over x).HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat AddCommGrpCat)]
63	%   [∀ x, HasWeakSheafify (J.over x) AddCommGrpCat]
64	%   [∀ x, (J.over x).WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat]
65	% These must be discharged (or assumed as instances) before the statements in
66	% this chapter typecheck for X.ringCatSheaf. The project already lives in the
67	% SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf world, so these should resolve; this is the one
68	% item to verify when the Lean file is scaffolded.
69	
70	The Mathlib predicates \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) and
71	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}\) are stated relative to a
72	Grothendieck topology \(J\) on a base category \(C\) and a sheaf of rings
73	\(R : \Sheaf(J, \RingCat)\). They require, for every object \(x : C\), three
74	instances on the slice topology \(J/x\) (Mathlib \(J.\mathtt{over}\ x\)):
75	\[
76	  (J/x).\mathtt{HasSheafCompose}(\forget_{\RingCat \to \AddCommGrpCat}),\quad
77	  \HasWeakSheafify(J/x, \AddCommGrpCat),\quad
78	  (J/x).\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}(\AddCommGrpCat).
79	\]
80	For \(X.\mathtt{Modules} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\ X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
81	these are the ambient site instances of the ringed space underlying \(X\). We
82	record their availability as a standing assumption for the chapter; verifying it
83	for \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is the single instance-resolution check the
84	formalisation must clear at entry, and is independent of the mathematical
85	content below.
86	
87	\begin{remark}
88	  \label{rem:lbc_site_instances}
89	  For the ringed-space site underlying \(X\) with sheaf of rings
90	  \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), every slice topology \(J/x\) carries the
91	  three instances \(\mathtt{HasSheafCompose}\), \(\HasWeakSheafify\) and
92	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) (for the forgetful functor
93	  \(\RingCat \to \AddCommGrpCat\)) demanded by
94	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}\). Under this assumption the
95	  predicate \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) is well-formed for every
96	  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\).
97	\end{remark}

99	\section{From pointwise triviality to a trivialising cover}
100	\label{sec:lbc_cover}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}\leanok
103	[Trivialising affine cover]
104	  \label{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
105	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.exists_trivializing_cover}
106	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
107	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Definition 17.25.1
108	  % "invertible module" (Tag 01CS), Section 17.25 (Tag 01CR)
109	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4046-L4059).
110	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
111	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. An
112	  %  {\it invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module} is a sheaf
113	  %  of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{L}$ such that
114	  %  the functor
115	  %  $$
116	  %  \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \longrightarrow \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X),\quad
117	  %  \mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
118	  %  $$
119	  %  is an equivalence of categories. We say that $\mathcal{L}$ is
120	  %  {\it trivial} if it is isomorphic as an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
121	  %  to $\mathcal{O}_X$."
122	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CS (Modules, Definition~17.25.1).}
123	  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\).
124	  Then there exist an index set \(I\), a family of affine open subschemes
125	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(X\) covering \(X\) (i.e. \(\bigcup_i U_i = X\)), and
126	  for each \(i\) an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules
127	  \[
128	    e_i \;:\; M|_{U_i} \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \struct{U_i}
129	  \]
130	  to the structure sheaf-as-module on \(U_i\).
131	\end{lemma}
132	
133	\begin{proof}
134	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
135	  Apply the hypothesis \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\) at every point \(x \in X\): it
136	  produces an affine open \(U_x \ni x\) and an isomorphism
137	  \(e_x : M|_{U_x} \cong \struct{U_x}\). Take the index set \(I := X\) (the
138	  underlying set of points), and to the index \(x\) assign the chosen
139	  neighbourhood \(U_x\) and the chosen isomorphism \(e_x\). The family
140	  \(\{U_x\}_{x \in X}\) covers \(X\) because each \(x\) lies in its own \(U_x\).
141	  This is exactly the data the definition supplies, packaged as an indexed
142	  family rather than a pointwise existential.
143	\end{proof}

145	\section{The trivial presentation on a chart}
146	\label{sec:lbc_chart_presentation}
147	
148	\begin{lemma}\leanok
149	[Finite free presentation on a chart]
150	  \label{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
151	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation}
152	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
153	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, section
154	  % "Modules of finite presentation" (\label{definition-finite-presentation})
155	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L1377-L1392).
156	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
157	  % "We say that $\mathcal{F}$ is of {\it finite presentation}
158	  %  if for every point $x \in X$ there exists an open neighbourhood
159	  %  $x\in U \subset X$, and  $n, m \in \mathbf{N}$ such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$
160	  %  is isomorphic to the cokernel of a map
161	  %  $$
162	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{j = 1, \ldots, m}
163	  %  \mathcal{O}_U
164	  %  \longrightarrow
165	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{i = 1, \ldots, n}
166	  %  \mathcal{O}_U
167	  %  $$"
168	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules, ``Modules of finite
169	  presentation''.}
170	  On each chart \(U_i\) of the trivialising cover of
171	  \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}, the restriction \(M|_{U_i}\) admits the
172	  trivial finite free presentation
173	  \[
174	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \struct{U_i} \;\longrightarrow\; M|_{U_i}
175	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
176	  \]
177	  i.e. \(M|_{U_i}\) is the cokernel of the zero map
178	  \(\bigoplus_{j \in \varnothing} \struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\)
179	  (the case \(m = 0\), \(n = 1\) of the finite-presentation pattern). The
180	  unit sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i}\) (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\))
181	  carries a canonical free presentation on one generator with no relations;
182	  this presentation is finite.
183	\end{lemma}
184	
185	\begin{proof}
186	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
187	  The structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,
188	  (U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is free of rank one on the single generator
189	  \(1\): the identity map \(\struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\) exhibits it as
190	  \(\bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), so the surjection
191	  \(\struct{U_i}^{\oplus 1} \twoheadrightarrow \struct{U_i}\) is an isomorphism
192	  and its kernel is zero, generated by the empty family. Thus \(\struct{U_i}\)
193	  has the canonical presentation with \(n = 1\) generators and \(m = 0\)
194	  relations; this canonical \(\mathtt{unit}\)-presentation is the rank-one free
195	  presentation, and Mathlib records it as \emph{finite} (the
196	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\) finite-presentation instance).
197	
198	  Concretely, in Lean the chart presentation is built by \emph{transport along
199	  the trivialisation iso}: take the canonical presentation \(\sigma\) of
200	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,(U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) and apply
201	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.Presentation.ofIsIso}\,e_i.\mathtt{hom}\) (Mathlib's
202	  transport of a presentation along an isomorphism \(e_i.\mathtt{hom}\) of the
203	  presented module) to obtain \((M.\mathtt{over}\,U_i).\mathtt{Presentation}\).
204	  Crucially, Mathlib supplies the instance making
205	  \((\mathtt{ofIsIso}\,f\,\sigma).\mathtt{IsFinite}\) hold whenever
206	  \(\sigma.\mathtt{IsFinite}\) holds; since the canonical \(\mathtt{unit}\)
207	  presentation is finite, the finiteness of the transported presentation is
208	  \emph{inferred automatically by typeclass resolution} on the
209	  \(\mathtt{ofIsIso}\) construction. No separate finiteness declaration
210	  (e.g.\ a \(\mathtt{chartPresentation\_isFinite}\) lemma) is needed: the
211	  \(\mathtt{IsFinite}\) datum rides along as an instance on
212	  \(\mathtt{chartPresentation}\) itself.
213	\end{proof}

215	\section{Finite presentation of a locally trivial line bundle}
216	\label{sec:lbc_finite_presentation}
217	
218	\begin{theorem}\leanok
219	[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finitely presented]
220	  \label{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
221	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFinitePresentation}
222	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation, rem:lbc_site_instances}
223	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
224	  % Lemma 17.25.4 (Tag 0B8M), invertible vs locally free of rank one
225	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
226	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
227	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
228	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
229	  %  If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
230	  %  the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case)."
231	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~0B8M (Modules, Lemma~17.25.4).}
232	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying \cref{rem:lbc_site_instances} and let
233	  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\). Then
234	  \(M\) is finitely presented: \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) holds in
235	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\).
236	\end{theorem}
237	
238	\begin{proof}
239	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
240	  By \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover} choose a trivialising affine cover
241	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) with isomorphisms \(e_i : M|_{U_i} \cong \struct{U_i}\).
242	  Assemble these into a \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) for \(M\): the index type
243	  is \(I\), the covering objects are the affine opens \(U_i\) (which cover \(X\)),
244	  and the presentation attached to index \(i\) is the finite free presentation of
245	  \(M|_{U_i}\) (equivalently, of the restriction \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U_i\))
246	  produced in \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}. Each such presentation's
247	  \(\mathtt{IsFinite}\) datum is \emph{not} supplied by hand: it is the
248	  automatic typeclass instance riding along on the
249	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.Presentation.ofIsIso}\) construction of
250	  \(\mathtt{chartPresentation}\) (\cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}), so the
251	  finiteness obligation of the assembled
252	  \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData.IsFinitePresentation}\) is discharged by
253	  inference rather than by a separate lemma. Feeding the resulting datum to
254	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk}\) — equivalently, the
255	  anonymous \(\langle\langle\dots\rangle\rangle\) constructor — gives
256	  \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\).
257	
258	  The only non-formal input is the trivialising cover; everything else is the
259	  definitional unwinding of finite presentation as a local property
260	  (Stacks, ``Modules of finite presentation''), since a rank-one free module is its own finite
261	  presentation. No stalk computation and no neighbourhood-spreading argument is
262	  used: the hypothesis already provides the local free models.
263	\end{proof}
264	
265	\begin{corollary}\leanok
266	[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finite type]
267	  \label{cor:lbc_isFiniteType}
268	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFiniteType}
269	  \uses{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
270	  Under the hypotheses of \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}, the locally
271	  trivial module \(M\) is of finite type (\(M.\mathtt{IsFiniteType}\)). This is
272	  the named result. Quasi-coherence (\(M.\mathtt{IsQuasicoherent}\)) follows
273	  automatically and needs no separate named theorem: it is supplied by the
274	  Mathlib instance \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation}\)
275	  from \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}.
276	\end{corollary}
277	
278	\begin{proof}
279	  \uses{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
280	  Finite presentation implies finite type by Mathlib's
281	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsFiniteTypeOfIsFinitePresentation}\), immediate
282	  from \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}; this is the content of the named
283	  declaration. Quasi-coherence is not separately stated in the Lean type — it is
284	  available as the Mathlib instance
285	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation}\), again
286	  immediate from \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}.
287	\end{proof}

289	\section{Rank one and flatness record}
290	\label{sec:lbc_rank_flat}
291	
292	% NOTE. Mathlib (at the pin) has no SheafOfModules-level "locally free of rank r"
293	% or "flat" predicate (verified absent: no SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFree). The
294	% rank-one and flatness facts are therefore recorded chart-locally, as the data
295	% the Quot embedding actually consumes, rather than as instances of a missing
296	% global predicate. Project-side predicates may be introduced if a downstream
297	% consumer needs them packaged; this is flagged for the plan agent.
298	
299	\begin{lemma}\leanok
300	[Chart-local rank one and flatness]
301	  \label{lem:lbc_rank_flat}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.chart_free_rank_one}
303	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
304	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, section
305	  % "Locally free sheaves" (\label{definition-locally-free}, "(finite)
306	  % locally free of rank $r$")
307	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2079-L2094).
308	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
309	  % "\item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it locally free} if for every
310	  %  point $x \in X$ there exist a set $I$ and an open
311	  %  neighbourhood $x \in U \subset X$
312	  %  such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$ is isomorphic to
313	  %  $\bigoplus_{i \in I} \mathcal{O}_X|_U$ as an $\mathcal{O}_X|_U$-module.
314	  %  \item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free} if we may
315	  %  choose the index sets $I$ to be finite.
316	  %  \item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free of rank $r$}
317	  %  if we may choose the index sets $I$ to have cardinality $r$."
318	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules, ``Locally free sheaves'' (finite
319	  locally free of rank \(r\)).}
320	  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial. The lemma records, at each
321	  point \(x \in X\), a chart \(U \ni x\) together with the trivialising iso
322	  \(M|_U \cong \struct{U}\) (to \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\)). This iso is the
323	  rank-one free \emph{model}: the Lean type states only the iso itself, since
324	  Mathlib has no \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\)-level rank or flatness predicate.
325	  Rank-one freeness and flatness are then \emph{consequences} of this iso — not
326	  separately recorded in the type — because \(\struct{U}\) is the free
327	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module of rank one and a flat module over itself (a ring is
328	  flat over itself), and both properties transport along \(e\). In the informal
329	  sense of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} this exhibits \(M\) as finite
330	  locally free of rank one and flat on the cover, but the formal content is
331	  exactly the chart-local iso.

334	\begin{proof}
335	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
336	  Immediate from the trivialisation: on \(U_i\), \(e_i\) identifies \(M|_{U_i}\)
337	  with \(\struct{U_i} = \bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), the free module
338	  of rank one. The structure sheaf-as-module is flat over itself, and an
339	  isomorphic module is flat; the index set \(\{*\}\) has cardinality one, so the
340	  rank is one. The properties are stated on the chart \(U_i\) because there is no
341	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\)-level locally free or flat predicate globally; the chart-local statements are
342	  what the Quot-scheme embedding consumes when it forms the Hilbert-polynomial
343	  input on a fibre.

1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application},
10	% \ref{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}) (read from
11	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
12	
13	\section{Setup and motivation}
14	
15	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
16	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
17	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
18	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
19	derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
20	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
21	available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
22	over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
23	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
24	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
25	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
26	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
27	
28	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
29	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
30	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
31	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
32	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
33	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
34	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
35	\[
36	  \begin{array}{ccc}
37	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
38	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
39	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
40	  \end{array}
41	\]
42	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
43	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
44	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
45	
46	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
47	
48	\begin{definition}
49	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
50	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
51	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
52	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
53	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
54	  % \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
55	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
57	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
58	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
59	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
60	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
61	  % the unit ideal, see
62	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
63	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
64	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
65	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
66	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
67	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
68	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
69	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
70	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
71	  simplicial object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in simplicial degree
72	  \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
73	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
74	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
75	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
76	  \[
77	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
78	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
79	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
80	  \]
81	  with faces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and degeneracies by
82	  the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree \(-1\) is the object
83	  on all of \(X\). When \(X\) is separated each intersection
84	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
85	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
86	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
87	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
88	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
89	\end{definition}
90	
91	\begin{definition}
92	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
93	  \label{def:cech_complex}
94	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
95	  \uses{def:cech_nerve}
96	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
97	  % \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
98	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
99	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
100	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
101	  % is identified with the complex
102	  % $$
103	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
104	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
105	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
106	  % \ldots
107	  % $$
108	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
109	  % \begin{equation}
110	  % \label{equation-extended}
111	  % 0 \to
112	  % M \to
113	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
114	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
115	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
116	  % \ldots
117	  % \end{equation}
118	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
119	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
120	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
121	  \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}.}
122	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
123	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
124	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
125	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
126	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
127	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
128	  in degree \(p\) it is
129	  \[
130	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
131	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
132	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
133	  \]
134	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
135	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
136	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
137	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
138	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
139	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
140	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
141	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
142	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
143	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
144	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
145	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
146	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
147	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
148	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
149	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
150	\end{definition}

230	  the standard cover is the complex of localisations

233	    \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \;\to\;

254	  yields \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\). \emph{(This is Serre
255	  vanishing for quasi-coherent sheaves on affine schemes.)}

310	% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}

312	% Cohomology, Lemma
313	% \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence-application}."
314	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Consider the Leray spectral sequence
315	% $E_2^{p, q} = H^p(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})$
316	% converging to $H^{p + q}(X, \mathcal{F})$, see
317	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-Leray}.

325	  The question is local on \(S\), and the formation of both sides commutes with
326	  restriction to opens of \(S\), so we may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) is
327	  affine. Because \(f\) is separated and quasi-compact, each finite intersection
328	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(f\) restricted to it is an affine
329	  morphism; by the affine acyclicity of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} the
330	  higher direct images of \(\mathcal{F}\) over each such intersection vanish, so the
331	  terms of the relative {\v C}ech complex carry no hidden higher cohomology.
332	
333	  Consider the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence of the cover
334	  \(\mathfrak{U}\), with \(E_2\)-page
335	  \(E_2^{p,q} = \check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\)
336	  converging to \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\). Since all intersections are affine,
337	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} forces the presheaf cohomology
338	  \(\underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})\) to vanish on the intersections for \(q > 0\), so
339	  the spectral sequence collapses to its row \(q = 0\): the {\v C}ech cohomology of
340	  \(\mathcal{F}\) for the cover \(\mathfrak{U}\) computes the sheaf cohomology,
341	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\) for all \(p\),
342	  and the same identification holds relatively, i.e.\ the cohomology sheaves of
343	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) compute the cohomology
344	  sheaves of \(Rf_*\mathcal{F}\).

346	  It remains to identify these cohomology sheaves with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\). Over
347	  the affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the Leray spectral sequence
348	  \(E_2^{p,q} = H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) \Rightarrow H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\)

351	  sequence therefore degenerates at \(E_2\), giving
352	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\). Combining with the previous
353	  paragraph, \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
354	  \cong R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules for all \(i \geq 0\).
355	\end{proof}

359	\begin{definition}
360	[Unconditional higher direct image via {\v C}ech]
361	  \label{def:cech_higher_direct_image}
362	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechHigherDirectImage}
363	  \uses{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
364	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
365	  % \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
366	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L843--854); the unconditional
367	  % packaging via the {\v C}ech complex is an Archon-original construction grounded
368	  % in this lemma and Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
369	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
370	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
371	  % Assume $S$ is affine.
372	  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
373	  % we have
374	  % $$
375	  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
376	  % $$
377	  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
378	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
379	  \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}; unconditional
380	  {\v C}ech packaging is Archon-original.}
381	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a
382	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Fix a finite affine open cover
383	  \(\mathfrak{U}\) of \(X\). The \emph{(unconditional) \(i\)-th higher direct
384	  image} is defined, for each \(i \geq 0\), as the \(i\)-th cohomology sheaf of the
385	  relative {\v C}ech complex,
386	  \[
387	    R^i f_*\mathcal{F} \;:=\;
388	      H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
389	      \in \operatorname{QCoh}(S).
390	  \]
391	  This requires \emph{no} hypothesis that the category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
392	  have enough injectives: the right-hand side is the cohomology of an explicit
393	  complex of quasi-coherent sheaves. By
394	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} the result is canonically isomorphic to
395	  the derived-functor higher direct image wherever the latter is defined, and in
396	  particular is independent of the chosen affine cover \(\mathfrak{U}\) up to
397	  canonical isomorphism: two affine covers admit a common affine refinement, and
398	  the induced refinement maps on {\v C}ech complexes are quasi-isomorphisms because
399	  both compute the same \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) by
400	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}. For \(i = 0\) one recovers the ordinary
401	  pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
402	\end{definition}

230	  %  the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case)."
231	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~0B8M (Modules, Lemma~17.25.4).}
232	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying \cref{rem:lbc_site_instances} and let
233	  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\). Then
234	  \(M\) is finitely presented: \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) holds in
235	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\).

238	\begin{proof}
239	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
240	  By \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover} choose a trivialising affine cover
241	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) with isomorphisms \(e_i : M|_{U_i} \cong \struct{U_i}\).
242	  Assemble these into a \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) for \(M\): the index type
243	  is \(I\), the covering objects are the affine opens \(U_i\) (which cover \(X\)),
244	  and the presentation attached to index \(i\) is the finite free presentation of
245	  \(M|_{U_i}\) (equivalently, of the restriction \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U_i\))
246	  produced in \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}. Each such presentation's
247	  \(\mathtt{IsFinite}\) datum is \emph{not} supplied by hand: it is the
248	  automatic typeclass instance riding along on the

254	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk}\) — equivalently, the
255	  anonymous \(\langle\langle\dots\rangle\rangle\) constructor — gives
256	  \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\).

259	  definitional unwinding of finite presentation as a local property
260	  (Stacks, ``Modules of finite presentation''), since a rank-one free module is its own finite

293	% or "flat" predicate (verified absent: no SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFree). The
294	% rank-one and flatness facts are therefore recorded chart-locally, as the data

300	[Chart-local rank one and flatness]
301	  \label{lem:lbc_rank_flat}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.chart_free_rank_one}
303	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
304	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, section
305	  % "Locally free sheaves" (\label{definition-locally-free}, "(finite)
306	  % locally free of rank $r$")
307	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2079-L2094).
308	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
309	  % "\item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it locally free} if for every
310	  %  point $x \in X$ there exist a set $I$ and an open
311	  %  neighbourhood $x \in U \subset X$
312	  %  such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$ is isomorphic to
313	  %  $\bigoplus_{i \in I} \mathcal{O}_X|_U$ as an $\mathcal{O}_X|_U$-module.
314	  %  \item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free} if we may
315	  %  choose the index sets $I$ to be finite.
316	  %  \item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free of rank $r$}
317	  %  if we may choose the index sets $I$ to have cardinality $r$."
318	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules, ``Locally free sheaves'' (finite
319	  locally free of rank \(r\)).}
320	  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial. The lemma records, at each
321	  point \(x \in X\), a chart \(U \ni x\) together with the trivialising iso
322	  \(M|_U \cong \struct{U}\) (to \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\)). This iso is the
323	  rank-one free \emph{model}: the Lean type states only the iso itself, since
324	  Mathlib has no \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\)-level rank or flatness predicate.
325	  Rank-one freeness and flatness are then \emph{consequences} of this iso — not
326	  separately recorded in the type — because \(\struct{U}\) is the free
327	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module of rank one and a flat module over itself (a ring is
328	  flat over itself), and both properties transport along \(e\). In the informal
329	  sense of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} this exhibits \(M\) as finite
330	  locally free of rank one and flat on the cover, but the formal content is
331	  exactly the chart-local iso.
332	\end{lemma}

335	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
336	  Immediate from the trivialisation: on \(U_i\), \(e_i\) identifies \(M|_{U_i}\)
337	  with \(\struct{U_i} = \bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), the free module
338	  of rank one. The structure sheaf-as-module is flat over itself, and an
339	  isomorphic module is flat; the index set \(\{*\}\) has cardinality one, so the
340	  rank is one. The properties are stated on the chart \(U_i\) because there is no
341	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\)-level locally free or flat predicate globally; the chart-local statements are
342	  what the Quot-scheme embedding consumes when it forms the Hilbert-polynomial
343	  input on a fibre.
344	\end{proof}
